content.text: The partial derivatives of a function f(x, y) are defined by:
content.math: \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = f_x = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0}\left[\frac{f(x+\Delta x,\,y)-f(x,\,y)}{\Delta x}\right]
content.text: which is the gradient of a curve within the surface where only x is allowed to change, y is effectively a constant and has no effect on the differentiation, and
content.math: \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = f_y = \lim_{\Delta y \to 0}\left[\frac{f(x,\,y+\Delta y)-f(x,\,y)}{\Delta y}\right]
content.text: which is the gradient of a curve within the surface where only y is allowed to change - x is effectively a constant and has no effect on the differentiation.
indicates that ordinary differentiation has been used to obtain the derivative with respect to x of a single-variable function, f(x). The expression
indicates the partial derivative with respect to x of a multivariable function, f(x, y, ...). These two expressions have different meanings and should not be confused.
